concept.title: XML Syntax Rules: Well-Formedness
concept.shortdesc: Every XML document must follow strict syntax rules to be considered well-formed — a parser will reject any document that violates them.

section.title: What does "well-formed" mean?
section.p: A well-formed XML document is one that follows all the syntax rules of the XML specification. This is the minimum requirement for any XML parser to accept a document. A document that is not well-formed is not XML at all — parsers will refuse to process it.
Well-formedness is different from validity. A well-formed document has correct syntax; a valid document also conforms to a schema (DTD or XSD). You will learn about validity in later chapters.

section.title: Rule 1: The XML declaration
section.p: Every XML document should begin with an XML declaration:

ul.li: version="1.0" — The XML version. Almost all XML in production uses version 1.0.
encoding="UTF-8" — The character encoding. UTF-8 is the default and recommended encoding.

section.note: The XML declaration must be the very first thing in the file — no blank lines, no spaces, no comments before it.

section.title: Rule 2: Exactly one root element
section.p: Every XML document must have exactly one root element that contains all other elements:
section.codeblock:
<!-- CORRECT: single root element --> <catalog> <product>Widget</product> <product>Gadget</product> </catalog>
<!-- WRONG: two root elements --> <product>Widget</product> <product>Gadget</product>

section.title: Rule 3: Every element must be closed
section.p: Unlike HTML, XML requires every opening tag to have a matching closing tag:
section.codeblock:
<!-- CORRECT --> <paragraph>Hello, world.</paragraph>
<!-- WRONG: unclosed element --> <paragraph>Hello, world.
section.p: Empty elements (elements with no content) can be written in two ways:

section.title: Rule 4: Elements must be properly nested
section.p: Child elements must be completely contained within their parent. Overlapping tags are not allowed:
section.codeblock:
<!-- CORRECT: proper nesting --> <bold><italic>important text</italic></bold>
<!-- WRONG: overlapping elements --> <bold><italic>important text</bold></italic>

section.title: Rule 5: XML is case-sensitive
section.p: Element and attribute names are case-sensitive. The opening and closing tags must use identical casing:
section.codeblock:
<!-- CORRECT --> <Title>My Book</Title>
<!-- WRONG: case mismatch --> <Title>My Book</title>
section.note: Convention: Use lowercase for element names and attributes in your own XML vocabularies. This is consistent with DITA, HTML5, and most modern XML standards.

section.title: Rule 6: Attribute values must be quoted
section.p: All attribute values must be enclosed in quotes — either single or double:
section.codeblock:
<!-- CORRECT --> <book category="fiction"> <book category='fiction'>
<!-- WRONG: unquoted attribute --> <book category=fiction>

ul.li: An element cannot have two attributes with the same name.
Attribute values cannot contain the < character directly — use < instead.

section.title: Rule 7: Special characters require entity references
section.p: Five characters have special meaning in XML and must be escaped when they appear in text content or attribute values:

row.entry: Double quote — used in attribute values

section.title: Comments and processing instructions
section.p: XML supports comments and processing instructions:
section.codeblock:
<!-- This is a comment. It is ignored by parsers. -->
<?xml-stylesheet type="text/xsl" href="transform.xsl"?>
section.p: Comments cannot contain the double-hyphen sequence (--) inside the comment body, and they cannot be nested.

section.title: CDATA sections: Escaping blocks of text
section.p: When you need to include a large block of text that contains many special characters (such as code examples), use a CDATA section:
Everything inside <![CDATA[ ... ]]> is treated as plain text — no entity escaping is needed.

section.title: Common well-formedness errors
section.p: When you are starting out, watch for these frequent mistakes:

ol.li: Mismatched case between opening and closing tags
Putting two root elements in one document

section.note: Use an XML-aware editor (VS Code with the XML extension, or Oxygen XML) — it will highlight well-formedness errors in real time as you type.
